result:
The rain is falling sideways again, the way it only does in this city when the wind gets bored and decides to play tricks. I’m watching it from the window of a tiny izakaya that smells like grilled squid and old wood. The neon outside bleeds pink and cyan across the puddles, turning the street into some kind of cyberpunk watercolor.

I’ve been thinking lately about how memory works like those puddles—reflecting things that aren’t really there anymore. Last week I found an old mixtape labeled “for rainy nights” in handwriting that isn’t mine. I have no idea who gave it to me. The first song is still perfect. The rest is forgettable. That feels like a metaphor for most relationships I’ve had.

There’s a cat asleep on the bar stool next to me. It’s missing half an ear and clearly judges my life choices. I respect that. Cats don’t pretend. They don’t send you memes at 2 a.m. pretending they’re fine when they’re not. They just exist with brutal honesty and occasional violence. We could learn from them.

I ordered too much sake again. The old man behind the counter keeps refilling my glass without asking, like he can see the particular brand of lonely that’s sitting on my shoulders tonight. Maybe he can. Bartenders in this city develop a sixth sense for it, like how sailors can smell a storm coming.

The rain hasn’t stopped. I think I’ll stay here until it does. Or until the cat decides I’m worthy of attention. Whichever comes first.

(Word count: 248)
